essence: Apparel/luxury is a brand-and-desire business: people pay for identity, status, and design - so brand equity is the core asset.
Two different games: fast/mass apparel competes on speed, price, and supply chain; luxury competes on scarcity, heritage, and pricing power.
Inventory and trend risk are brutal in fashion (markdowns kill); luxury deliberately limits supply to protect desirability.
Gross margins can be high (especially luxury) but trend misses and discounting destroy them.
Brand heat, distribution control, and (for luxury) scarcity discipline decide who wins.

definition.oneLiner: Companies that design, brand, and sell clothing, footwear, accessories, and luxury goods - selling identity and design as much as product.
definition.jobToBeDone: Help people express identity, status, and taste - and meet the practical need to be clothed - through branded products.

valueChain.profitPools: Profit pools concentrate in strong brands with pricing power - luxury houses earn the richest margins via scarcity and heritage; mass apparel profit sits with fast, efficient supply chains; commoditised, discounted product is thin.
valueChain.powerShift: Value is shifting from wholesale + department stores -> brand-controlled DTC (own stores + online) -> digital/social brand building and (for luxury) experiential + resale, as brands seek to own the customer and the price.

variants: Fast fashion (speed, low price, high volume)

unitEconomics.unitOfRevenue: Revenue per unit (price x volume), with full-price vs markdown mix decisive
unitEconomics.revenueDrivers: Units x Price. Growth comes from volume, price/premiumisation, and crucially the full-price sell-through rate; brand heat drives all three.

costStructure.shape: COGS (materials + manufacturing, often outsourced) plus brand/marketing and retail/distribution; the swing factor is markdowns - product sold at full price is highly profitable, discounted product can be loss-making.
costStructure.leverage: High operating leverage on a hit (great product at full price); brands with pricing power and full-price discipline (esp. luxury) earn outsized margins, but a trend miss or over-production destroys them.

moats: Brand equity & heritage (esp. luxury - decades to build)
competitionAndMoats.concentration: Luxury is concentrated in a few global conglomerates (LVMH, Kering, Richemont); mass/fast fashion has a few global giants (Inditex, H&M, Shein) plus huge fragmentation and regional brands.

structuralForces.regulation: Moderate - labour & supply-chain (sweatshop/ESG) scrutiny, sustainability/circularity rules, import duties, IP/counterfeiting enforcement.
structuralForces.cyclicality: Discretionary and trend-driven; luxury is somewhat resilient (wealthy buyers) but exposed to economic cycles and tourism/FX; fashion is highly seasonal.
structuralForces.substitution: Resale/second-hand (booming), rental, fast fashion vs premium trade-offs, and counterfeits (for luxury).
structuralForces.supplierBuyerPower: Manufacturers (often low-cost countries) have limited power; for luxury the brand holds power over consumers; for mass, consumers and large retailers hold sway.

aiPenetration.maturity: Medium - strong in demand/trend forecasting and personalisation; generative AI now in design and marketing.
aiPenetration.valueChainImpact: AI's biggest value is taming inventory/trend risk (forecasting, sizing - fewer markdowns and returns) and personalising the experience; generative AI speeds design and content but raises authenticity/brand questions in luxury.
opportunities: Fewer markdowns & returns via better forecasting/sizing

ranges: Full-price sell-through: higher is better (luxury very high)
Inventory turns: fast fashion high, luxury lower by design
Markdown rate: the key margin swing
DTC mix: rising across the board
benchmarks.note: DIRECTIONAL - rules of thumb, not audited figures. Verify before quoting.

keyTerms.Full-price sell-through: Share of product sold at full price vs discounted - the key profit driver.
keyTerms.Markdown: Discount to clear stock - directly destroys margin.
keyTerms.DTC: Direct-to-consumer (own stores/online) vs selling through wholesale/department stores.
keyTerms.Fast fashion: Rapid, cheap, trend-chasing apparel with very short cycles.
keyTerms.Scarcity (luxury): Deliberately limiting supply to protect desirability and price.
keyTerms.Clienteling: High-touch personalised service to top customers.
keyTerms.Resale: Second-hand market - growing fast, both threat and opportunity.

questionsToAskFirst: What's the full-price sell-through and markdown rate?
Mass, premium, or luxury - and is the brand hot or cooling?
DTC vs wholesale mix and trajectory?
Is pricing power real (luxury) or discount-dependent (mass)?
rookieTraps: Reading revenue without the full-price vs markdown split
Treating luxury and fast fashion as one economic model
Chasing growth that dilutes a luxury brand's scarcity

whatsUniqueHere: Apparel and luxury sell desire, not just product - which makes the brand the entire asset and pricing power the whole game. Luxury is almost a paradox: it grows by restraining supply, because scarcity is what justifies the price; push volume too hard and you kill the magic. Mass fashion plays the opposite game - speed and cost - and the universal enemy of both is the markdown rack, where mis-read trends and over-production go to destroy margin.

indiaLandscape.marketStructure: A large, young, brand-hungry market: domestic apparel leaders (Aditya Birla Fashion, Trent's Zudio/Westside, Reliance's brands), global fast fashion (Zara, H&M, Uniqlo), a booming D2C layer, and a fast-growing (still nascent) luxury segment concentrated in metros.
indiaLandscape.note: Semi-perishable - players & policy specifics refresh periodically.

currentState.note: PERISHABLE - refresh via research before quoting. Figures approximate.
currentState.marketStructure: India apparel: value fashion (Trent's Zudio, Reliance) booming and highly profitable; global fast fashion expanding; D2C brands scaling via quick-commerce/online; ethnic & wedding wear (Manyavar) strong; luxury growing in metros but still small. Markdown discipline and the DTC shift are the margin themes.
keyTrends: AI in sizing/forecasting to cut returns
